facts.text: Olaudah Equiano was also known as Gustavus Vassa.
Equiano wrote his own narrative.
Equiano was torn from his family by the slave trade.
Equiano regards his introduction to Christianity as compensation for his suffering.
Equiano believes Britain has liberal sentiments and humanity.
Equiano was born in the year 1745.
Equiano was born in a province called Eboe in a vale named Essaka.
Equiano had never heard of white men or Europeans before his kidnapping.
Equiano had never heard of the sea before his kidnapping.
Equiano's father was a chief or elder styled Embrenche.
Equiano's father bore the mark of Embrenche on his forehead.
Equiano was destined by his parents to receive the mark of Embrenche.
Equiano had a brother who received the mark of Embrenche.
Equiano had a sister who was kidnapped with him.
Equiano regards himself as a particular favourite of Heaven.
Equiano acknowledges the mercies of Providence in every occurrence of his life.
Equiano wrote his narrative at the request of numerous friends.
Equiano's motive for publishing was to promote the interests of humanity.
Equiano does not aspire to literary reputation or immortality.
Equiano's people are almost a nation of dancers, musicians, and poets.
Equiano's people practice a plurality of wives, though seldom more than two.
Equiano's people consider the honour of the marriage bed sacred.
Equiano's people are jealous of the fidelity of their wives.
Equiano's people punish adultery with slavery or death.
Equiano's people practice the law of retaliation in justice.
Equiano's people practice betrothal of young people by their parents.
Equiano's people mark married women with a cotton string tied around the waist.
Equiano's people celebrate marriages with bonfires, music, and dancing.
Equiano's people give dowries consisting of land, slaves, cattle, and household goods.
The kingdom of Benin is the most considerable kingdom in Guinea.
Equiano's province was remote and fertile.
Equiano's subjection to the king of Benin was little more than nominal.
The chiefs or elders of Equiano's place conducted the government.
Equiano's mother's lamentations heightened the terror of gloomy places.
The people in Equiano's community computed the year from the day the sun crosses the line.
On the evening of the sun crossing the line, there was a general shout throughout the land.
The people made noise with rattles and held up their hands to heaven for a blessing.
Children whom wise men foresaw would be fortunate were presented to different people.
Many people came to see Equiano for this purpose when he was young.
Offerings were made at full moons and at harvest time.
Offerings were sometimes made when young animals were killed.
Offerings made by a head of family served for the whole family.
Offerings were eaten with bitter herbs.
Equiano's community practiced circumcision like the Jews.
Children in Equiano's community were named from events, circumstances, or foreboding at the time of their birth.
Equiano was named Olaudah, which signifies vicissitude or fortune.
The name Olaudah also means one favoured, and having a loud voice and well spoken.
Equiano's community never polluted the name of the object of their adoration.
Equiano's community was totally unacquainted with swearing and terms of abuse.
The natives of Equiano's part of Africa were extremely cleanly.
Cleanliness was part of religion in Equiano's community.
Equiano's community had many purifications and washings like the Jews.
Those who touched the dead were obliged to wash and purify themselves.
Women at certain times were forbidden to enter a dwelling-house or touch any person or food.
Equiano was so fond of his mother he could not avoid touching her during forbidden periods.
Equiano was kept out with his mother in a little house until offering was made and they were purified.
Equiano's community had priests and magicians or wise men.
The wise men were held in great reverence by the people.
The wise men calculated time and foretold events.
The wise men were called Ah-affoe-way-cah, which signifies calculators or yearly men.
The wise men wore their beards.
When wise men died they were succeeded by their sons.
Most of the wise men's implements and things of value were interred with them.
Pipes and tobacco were put into the grave with the corpse.
The corpse was always perfumed and ornamented.
Animals were offered in sacrifice to the dead wise men.
Only those of the same profession or tribe accompanied the funerals of wise men.
The wise men buried the dead after sunset.
The wise men always returned from the grave by a different way from that which they went.
The magicians were also doctors or physicians.
The magicians practiced bleeding by cupping.
The magicians were very successful in healing wounds and expelling poisons.
The magicians had an extraordinary method of discovering jealousy, theft, and poisoning.
The magicians' success derived from their unbounded influence over the credulity and superstition of the people.
A virgin had been poisoned but it was not known by whom.
The doctors ordered the corpse to be taken up and carried to the grave.
When the bearers raised the poisoned virgin's corpse on their shoulders, they seemed seized with a sudden impulse.
The bearers ran to and fro unable to stop themselves.
The bearers passed through thorns and prickly bushes unhurt.
The corpse fell from the bearers close to a house and defaced it.
The owner of the house immediately confessed to the poisoning.
The natives were extremely cautious about poison.
When buying eatable, the seller kissed it all round to show it was not poisoned.
The same kissing was done when any meat or drink was presented, particularly to a stranger.
Equiano's community had serpents of different kinds.
Some serpents were esteemed ominous when they appeared in houses.
The ominous serpents were never molested.
Two ominous snakes, each as thick as the calf of a man's leg, crept into Equiano's mother's night-house.
The ominous snakes resembled a dolphin in color.
The ominous snakes coiled themselves into folds.
The ominous snakes crowed like a cock.
Equiano was desired by wise men to touch the ominous snakes.
Equiano touched the ominous snakes to be interested in the good omens.
The ominous snakes were quite harmless and would tamely suffer themselves to be handled.
The ominous snakes were put into a large open earthen pan and set on one side of the highway.
Some of Equiano's community's snakes were poisonous.
A poisonous snake crossed the road when Equiano was standing on it.
The poisonous snake passed between Equiano's feet without offering to touch him.
Many people were surprised that the poisonous snake did not touch Equiano.
The wise men accounted these incidents as remarkable omens in Equiano's favor.
Equiano's mother and the rest of the people accounted these incidents as remarkable omens in Equiano's favor.
Equiano's community's manners and customs had a strong analogy with those of the Jews before they reached the Land of Promise.
Equiano's community's manners and customs had a strong analogy with those of the patriarchs in their pastoral state.
Dr. Gill deduced the pedigree of the Africans from Afer and Afra, the descendants of Abraham by Keturah.
Dr. John Clarke, formerly Dean of Sarum, ascribed African origins to Abraham.
Scripture chronology confirms the resemblance between Africans and Israelites.
Equiano's community's government was conducted by chiefs or judges, wise men and elders.
The head of a family in Equiano's community enjoyed similar authority to that of Abraham and the patriarchs.
The law of retaliation obtained almost universally in Equiano's community as with the Israelites.
Equiano's community had circumcision, sacrifices and burnt-offerings, washings and purifications like the Israelites.
Equiano did not presume to account for the difference of colour between Eboan Africans and modern Jews.
Mr. T. Clarkson ascertained the cause of color difference in his Essay on the Slavery and Commerce of the Human Species.
Dr. Mitchel related that Spaniards who inhabited America under the torrid zone became as dark colored as native Indians of Virginia.
A Portuguese settlement at Mitomba, a river in Sierra Leona, had inhabitants bred from a mixture of Portuguese discoverers and natives.
The inhabitants of Mitomba became perfect negroes in complexion and woolly quality of hair.
The inhabitants of Mitomba retained a smattering of the Portuguese language.
The complexions of the same persons vary in different climates.
Equiano believed understanding is not confined to feature or colour.
Equiano's ancestors were once uncivilized and even barbarous.
Equiano believed Nature did not make Europeans inferior to their sons.
Equiano believed Europeans should not have been made slaves.
Equiano believed slavery depresses the mind and extinguishes all its fire and every noble sentiment.
Equiano believed refined people possess advantages over those who are rude and uncultivated.
Equiano's country's manners and customs had been implanted in him with great care.
The manners and customs made an impression on Equiano's mind which time could not erase.
Equiano's adversity and variety of fortune served only to rivet and record his country's customs.
Equiano still looked back with pleasure on the first scenes of his life.
Equiano's pleasure in looking back was for the most part mingled with sorrow.
Equiano's father had many slaves and a numerous family.
Seven of Equiano's father's children lived to grow up, including Equiano and a sister.
Equiano had a sister who was the only daughter.
Equiano was the youngest of the sons.
Equiano became the greatest favourite with his mother.
Equiano was always with his mother.
Equiano's mother used to take particular pains to form his mind.
Equiano was trained up from his earliest years in the art of war.
Equiano's daily exercise was shooting and throwing javelins.
Equiano's mother adorned him with emblems after the manner of their greatest warriors.
Equiano grew up till he was turned the age of eleven.
When grown people were gone far in the fields to labour, the children assembled together to play.
Some children used to get up a tree to look out for assailants or kidnappers.
Kidnappers sometimes took opportunities of parents' absence to attack and carry off children.
One day Equiano was watching at the top of a tree in his yard.
Equiano saw one of those people come into the yard of his next neighbour but one to kidnap.
There were many stout young people in the neighbour's yard.
Equiano gave the alarm of the rogue.
The rogue was surrounded by the stoutest of the children.
The rogue was entangled with cords so he could not escape.
The grown people came and secured the rogue.
Later Equiano was attacked and carried off when none of the grown people were nigh.
One day all of Equiano's people were gone out to their works as usual.
Only Equiano and his dear sister were left to mind the house.
Two men and a woman got over Equiano's walls.
The two men and woman seized Equiano and his sister.
The attackers did not give Equiano and his sister time to cry out or make resistance.
The attackers stopped their mouths and ran off with them into the nearest wood.
The attackers tied their hands.
The attackers carried them as far as they could till night came on.
They reached a small house where the robbers halted for refreshment and spent the night.
Equiano and his sister were then unbound.
Equiano and his sister were unable to take any food.
Equiano and his sister were quite overpowered by fatigue and grief.
Equiano and his sister's only relief was some sleep.
Equiano and his sister were seized by two men and a woman who invaded their home while the adults were away.
Equiano and his sister were carried into the woods with their mouths stopped and hands tied.
Equiano and his sister were separated after being captured, causing him severe distress.
Equiano was passed through many masters before reaching a chieftain's household in a pleasant country.
Equiano's first master was a smith who worked with gold.
Equiano's principal employment was working his master's bellows.
Equiano was treated well by his first master's household, particularly by the first wife.
Equiano's first master's household spoke the same language as his own people.
Equiano observed the sun's position to determine the direction of his father's house.
Equiano was not permitted to eat with free-born children despite being their companion.
Equiano valued liberty greatly.
Equiano's mother taught him never to tell lies.
Equiano accidentally killed a chicken by throwing a pebble at it.
Equiano told the truth about killing the chicken because his mother forbade lying.
Equiano had seldom been beaten at home.
Equiano hid in the bushes to avoid punishment for killing the chicken.
Equiano learned from the searchers that returning home would be hopeless due to distance and the intricate way.
Equiano feared snakes in the woods.
Equiano spent the night in the ashes of his master's kitchen.
Equiano's master slightly reprimanded him but ordered he not be ill-treated.
Equiano's master's only daughter died, causing the master severe grief.
Equiano was sold after his master's daughter's death.
Equiano acquired two or three different languages while traveling through Africa.
Equiano was reunited with his sister during his travels.
Equiano's sister was torn from him again the morning after their reunion.
Equiano was deeply affected by his sister's fate and worried about her suffering.
Equiano was sold to a merchant in the town of Tinmah.
Equiano first tasted cocoa-nuts in Tinmah.
Equiano first tasted sugar-cane in Tinmah.
Equiano was purchased by a wealthy widow and her son for one hundred and seventy-two shells.
Equiano was washed and perfumed at his new mistress's house.
Equiano ate with his mistress and her son, which astonished him.
Equiano's young master allowed him to eat first because Equiano was the eldest.
Equiano spent about two months with the widow's family.
Equiano began to think he would be adopted into the widow's family.
Equiano was suddenly sold away from the widow's household early one morning.
Equiano encountered people whose customs, manners, and language differed from his own.
Equiano encountered people who did not circumcise and ate without washing their hands.
Equiano was amazed by people who cooked in iron pots and possessed European cutlasses and cross bows.
Equiano observed that some peoples ornamented themselves with scars and filed their teeth sharp.
Equiano refused to allow himself to be ornamented with scars or sharp filed teeth.
Equiano had never seen water larger than a pond or rivulet before reaching a large river.
Equiano saw canoes on a large river where people appeared to live with their household utensils and provisions.
Equiano was transported by ship to a merchant's yard where enslaved Africans were confined together.
Equiano saw multi-story houses for the first time in the merchant's yard.
Equiano saw people on horseback for the first time and initially thought it was magical.
Equiano understood other Africans from distant parts of Africa despite language differences.
Equiano learned that some African regions had horses larger than those he initially saw.
Equiano was sold at auction along with other enslaved people after a drum signal.
Equiano witnessed families and friends being separated during the slave auction.
Equiano observed brothers being sold in different lots and heard their cries at parting.
Equiano was shipped to North America on a sloop after being separated from his countrymen.
Equiano received better treatment during the passage to North America than during the voyage from Africa.
Equiano was landed near Virginia where he saw few native Africans and no one he could communicate with.
Equiano worked weeding grass and gathering stones on a plantation.
Equiano was extremely miserable and wished for death while isolated on the plantation.
Equiano was sent to fan a gentleman in his house and was frightened by unfamiliar objects.
Equiano saw an enslaved Black woman wearing an iron muzzle that prevented her from eating or drinking.
Equiano was astonished by a watch hanging on the chimney and thought it might tell on him.
Equiano thought a picture in the room was magical or a way to preserve dead great men.
Equiano was called Jacob on the plantation and Michael on the African snow ship.
Captain Michael Henry Pascal purchased Equiano from Mr. Campbell.
Captain Pascal intended Equiano as a present for friends in England.
Equiano was transported to England on the Industrious Bee, a merchant ship loaded with tobacco.
Equiano thought the crew on the Industrious Bee treated him more kindly than any white people he had previously encountered.
Equiano was told by crew members that they were taking him back to his own country.
Captain Pascal renamed Equiano Gustavus Vassa against his wishes.
Equiano initially refused to answer to the name Gustavus Vassa and was beaten for his refusal.
Equiano eventually submitted to being called Gustavus Vassa.
The ship had a very long passage with short provisions.
Equiano received only one pound and a half of bread per week and the same quantity of meat during the voyage.
Equiano received one quart of water per day during the voyage.
The captain and crew jokingly told Equiano they would kill and eat him.
Equiano believed the crew's jokes about eating him were serious threats.
The crew caught a large shark but only cut off a small part of the tail and threw the rest overboard.
Richard Baker was a young American lad about four or five years older than Equiano.
Richard Baker had received an excellent education and had an amiable temperament.
Richard Baker showed Equiano great partiality and attention soon after Equiano boarded the ship.
Equiano grew extremely fond of Richard Baker.
Equiano and Richard Baker became inseparable companions for two years.
Richard Baker was Equiano's constant companion and instructor.
Equiano and Richard Baker lay in each other's bosoms during times of great distress.
Richard Baker died in 1759 while on board his majesty's ship the Preston in the Archipelago.
Equiano deeply regretted Richard Baker's death.
Richard Baker was fifteen years old when he died.
Richard Baker had a mind superior to prejudice.
Richard Baker was not ashamed to be the friend and instructor of Equiano despite Equiano being ignorant, a stranger, of different complexion, and a slave.
Captain Pascal lodged in Richard Baker's mother's house in America.
Captain Pascal respected Richard Baker very much and made him always eat with him in the cabin.
Captain Pascal jokingly told Richard Baker that he would kill Equiano to eat.
Equiano was alarmed for Richard Baker's safety when the captain made jokes about killing him.
Equiano would watch to see if the crew was going to kill Richard Baker.
A man was lost overboard during the voyage.
Equiano thought the crew was going to make an offering with him when the man went overboard.
Equiano believed the Ruler of the seas was angry and expected to be offered up to appease him.
Equiano could not close his eyes to rest the night a man went overboard.
Equiano saw large fish called grampusses that blew water on the ship's deck.
Equiano believed the grampusses were rulers of the sea.
Equiano thought the white people were angry with the grampusses because they made no offerings.
The wind died away and a calm ensued when Equiano saw the grampusses.
Equiano hid himself in the fore part of the ship in fear of being offered up to the grampusses.
Richard Baker told Equiano that the grampusses would swallow anybody.
The captain lit a barrel of pitch and put it over the side into the water to distract the grampusses.
The grampusses followed the barrel of pitch and disappeared.
The ship arrived at Falmouth after a passage of thirteen weeks.
Equiano was very much struck with the buildings and pavement of the streets in Falmouth.
Equiano arrived in England in the beginning of spring 1757.
Equiano was near twelve years of age when he arrived in England.
Equiano saw snow for the first time on the ship's deck in Falmouth.
Equiano initially thought the snow was salt.
Equiano asked the mate to come see the salt that had been thrown on the deck overnight.
Equiano found the snow very cold when he picked it up.
Equiano tasted the snow when the mate asked him to.
Equiano was surprised beyond measure when he tasted the snow.
Equiano did not understand what snow was when the mate told him.
Equiano was struck with the buildings and pavement of the streets in Falmouth.
Equiano had never seen snow before arriving in England.
Equiano initially thought snow was salt.
Equiano had never been to church before arriving in England.
Equiano was amazed at the church service.
Equiano had a friend named Dick who served as his interpreter.
Equiano was pleased that white people did not sell one another as Africans did.
Equiano thought white people were happier than Africans.
Equiano was astonished at the wisdom of white people.
Equiano was amazed that white people did not make sacrifices or offerings.
Equiano thought African women were more modest and shamefaced than white women.
Equiano observed his master and Dick reading.
Equiano had great curiosity to learn how to read.
Equiano attempted to talk to books, hoping they would answer him.
Equiano's master lodged at the house of a gentleman in Falmouth.
The gentleman's daughter, about six or seven years old, became very fond of Equiano.
Equiano and the gentleman's daughter ate together and had servants wait on them.
Equiano was caressed by the Falmouth family.
Equiano cried when asked if he would stay behind with the gentleman's daughter.
Equiano was sent on board ship by stealth one night.
The ship sailed for Guernsey.
Guernsey was in part owned by a merchant named Nicholas Doberry.
Equiano was glad he did not have his face scarred like some African nations.
Equiano's master placed him to board with one of his mates in Guernsey.
The mate had a wife and family in Guernsey.
Equiano's friend Dick remained with him in Guernsey.
The mate had a little daughter named Mary, aged about five or six years.
Equiano tried to wash his face to match Mary's complexion but was unsuccessful.
Equiano became mortified at the difference in complexions between himself and Mary.
The mate's wife treated Equiano with great kindness and attention.
The mate's wife taught Equiano as she taught her own child.
Equiano remained in Guernsey until the summer of 1757.
Equiano's master was appointed first lieutenant of His Majesty's ship the Roebuck.
Equiano was very much frightened by the press-gang.
Equiano hid under a hencoop when the press-gang came aboard.
The press-gang found Equiano and held him up by the heels.
The mate came to Equiano's assistance during the press-gang incident.
Equiano was amazed at the quantity of men and guns on the Roebuck.
Equiano's knowledge increased and his apprehensions diminished over time.
Equiano began to long for a battle after being on the Roebuck for some time.
Equiano's griefs wore away while on the Roebuck.
There were a number of boys on board the Roebuck.
Equiano and the other boys spent a great part of their time in play.
Equiano remained on the Roebuck for a considerable time.
The Roebuck made several cruises and visited a variety of places.
The Roebuck visited Holland twice.
Equiano fought with a white boy for the first time on the Roebuck.
Equiano had a bloody nose for the first time after fighting with a white boy.
Equiano fought for more than an hour in his first fight with a white boy.
The captain and ship's company encouraged Equiano to fight.
The ship went to Leith in Scotland.
The ship went to the Orkneys from Leith.
Equiano was surprised at seeing scarcely any night in the Orkneys.
The ship sailed with a great fleet of soldiers for England from the Orkneys.
The ship never came to an engagement while cruising off the coast of France.
The ship chased many vessels and took in all seventeen prizes.
Equiano learned many manoeuvres of the ship during the cruise.
Equiano was made to fire the guns several times.
Off Havre de Grace, the ship met with a fine large French-built frigate.
The ship prepared for fighting with the frigate.
The frigate hoisted English colours just as the word of command was given to fire.
The frigate proved to be the Ambuscade man of war.
The ship returned to Portsmouth without having been in any action.
Equiano saw Admiral Byng several times during his trial.
Equiano's master left the ship and went to London for promotion.
Equiano and Dick were put on board the Savage sloop of war.
The Savage went to assist in bringing off the St. George man of war.
Equiano and Dick were sent on shore at Deal after staying a few weeks on the Savage.
Equiano's master sent for Equiano and Dick to come to London.
Equiano had long desired to see London.
Equiano and Dick got into a waggon and came to London.
Equiano was received by Mr. Guerin, a relation of his master.
Mr. Guerin had two sisters who took much notice and great care of Equiano.
Equiano had chilblains to such a degree that he could not stand for several months.
Equiano was sent to St. George's Hospital.
The doctors wanted to cut off Equiano's left leg at different times.
Equiano said he would rather die than have his leg cut off.
Equiano recovered without the operation.
The small-pox broke out on Equiano after he recovered from chilblains.
Equiano's master was promoted to be first lieutenant of the Preston man of war.
Equiano and Dick were sent on board the Preston.
The Preston went to Holland to bring over the late Duke to England.
A young man on the Preston damned his eyes and lost his left eye within six or seven days.
Equiano considered the young man's loss of his eye a judgment of God.
Equiano's master was appointed a lieutenant on board the Royal George.
Equiano's master wished him to stay on the Preston to learn the French horn.
The Preston was ordered for Turkey.
Equiano told his master that leaving him behind would break his heart.
Equiano's master took him to the Royal George.
Dick remained on the Preston when Equiano left.
Equiano embraced Dick at parting for the last time.
The Royal George was the largest ship Equiano had ever seen.
The Royal George had men, women, and children of every denomination on board.
The Royal George had shops or stalls of every kind of goods.
Equiano felt cast without a friend on the Royal George because Dick was not with him.
Equiano's master got an appointment to be sixth lieutenant of the Namur.
The Namur was at Spithead, fitting up for Vice-admiral Boscawen.
Vice-admiral Boscawen was going with a large fleet on an expedition against Louisbourgh.
The crew of the Royal George were turned over to the Namur.
The flag of Admiral Boscawen was hoisted on the Namur.
There was a very great fleet of men of war assembled for the expedition.
Equiano was in hopes of being gratified with a sea-fight.
Admiral Cornish's fleet was destined for the East Indies.
The two fleets sailed together for several days and then parted.
The ship steered for America but was driven to Teneriffe by contrary winds.
Equiano was struck with the noted peak of Teneriffe.
The peak of Teneriffe resembled a sugar-loaf.
The ship remained in sight of Teneriffe for some days.
The ship proceeded to America and got into a harbour called St. George in Halifax.
There was fish in great plenty in St. George harbour.
Different men of war and transport ships with soldiers joined the fleet at Halifax.
The fleet sailed for Cape Breton in Nova Scotia.
General Wolfe was on board the Namur.
General Wolfe's affability made him highly esteemed and beloved by all the men.
General Wolfe honoured Equiano and other boys with marks of his notice.
General Wolfe saved Equiano once from a flogging for fighting with a young gentleman.
The fleet arrived at Cape Breton in the summer of 1758.
The soldiers were to be landed to make an attack upon Louisbourgh.
Equiano's master had some part in superintending the landing.
Equiano saw an encounter between the men and the enemy at Cape Breton.
The French were posted on the shore to receive the landing.
The French disputed the landing for a long time.
The French were driven from their trenches and a complete landing was effected.
The troops pursued the French as far as the town of Louisbourgh.
Many were killed on both sides in the action at Cape Breton.
A lieutenant of the Princess Amelia was shot through the mouth by a musquet ball.
Equiano had in his hand the scalp of an Indian king killed in the engagement.
The scalp was taken off by a Highlander.
The Indian king's ornaments were made of feathers.
The land forces laid siege to the town of Louisbourgh.
The French men of war were blocked up in the harbour by the fleet.
Batteries played upon the French men of war from the land.
Equiano witnessed an encounter between English and French forces at Louisbourg.
The French were initially posted on shore to defend against the English landing.
English troops pursued the French to the town of Louisbourg after driving them from their trenches.
Many soldiers were killed on both sides during the landing action.
A lieutenant of the Princess Amelia was shot through the mouth by a musket ball during the landing.
Equiano held the scalp of an Indian king killed in the engagement, which had been taken by a Highlander.
English land forces laid siege to Louisbourg while the French fleet was blocked in the harbor.
English batteries fired upon French ships in the harbor, setting some on fire.
Captain George Balfour commanded the Ætna fire-ship.
English boats attacked and boarded two remaining French men of war in the harbor.
The English set fire to a seventy-gun French ship.
The English captured a sixty-four gun ship called the Bienfaisant.
Captain Balfour noticed Equiano and liked him so much that he asked Equiano's master to let him have Equiano.
Equiano's master refused to part with him.
Equiano would not have left his master under any consideration.
Louisbourg was eventually taken by the English.
After Louisbourg's capture, Equiano had more liberty and went often on shore.
A beautiful procession of admirals and captains in decorated barges took place on the water.
The French governor and his lady came aboard the English ship to dine.
The English ships were dressed with colors from the topgallant-mast head to the deck.
Admiral Boscawen sailed with part of the fleet for England, leaving other ships behind.
During the passage home in winter, the English fleet encountered seven large French men of war in the channel.
The French fleet gave the English ships a broadside as they passed by.
The English ships were unprepared for battle, with their lower and middle deck guns housed in.
The Royal William and Somerset were the sternmost English ships and returned fire.
The French squadron was commanded by Mons. Conflans.
The English fleet prepared for battle and pursued the French fleet all night.
At daylight, the English saw six large French ships of the line and an English East Indiaman prize.
The English chased the French fleet all day until late afternoon.
The English came within musket shot of a seventy-four gun French ship.
The French Commodore's ship lost its foretopmast during the chase.
The French ships escaped and were out of sight by the next day.
The English captured only the old Indiaman called Carnarvon from the encounter.
The Namur ran aground at St. Helen's near the end of 1758-9.
Another large ship also ran aground at St. Helen's.
The English lightened the ships by starting water and tossing things overboard.
The ships were refloated without damage.
The fleet stayed briefly at Spithead and then went to Portsmouth harbor to refit.
The admiral went to London after Portsmouth.
Equiano's master and Equiano followed the admiral to London with a press-gang.
Equiano had spent between two and three years in England by this point.
A great part of Equiano's time in England had been spent at sea.
Equiano became inured to sea service.
Equiano's master treated him extremely well.
Equiano felt great attachment and gratitude to his master.
Equiano grew a stranger to terror from the various scenes he witnessed on shipboard.
Equiano became almost an Englishman in respect to fearlessness.
Equiano was filled with alarm at the first sight of Europeans and their actions.
Equiano's fear wore away as he came to know the Europeans.
Equiano could speak English tolerably well.
Equiano understood everything that was said to him.
Equiano felt quite easy with the English and relished their society and manners.
Equiano no longer looked upon Europeans as spirits but as men superior to Africans.
Equiano desired to resemble the English and imitate their manners.
Equiano embraced every occasion of improvement.
Equiano treasured up every new thing he observed in his memory.
Equiano had long wished to be able to read and write.
Equiano took every opportunity to gain instruction in reading and writing.
Equiano had made very little progress in reading and writing before going to London.
When Equiano went to London with his master, he had an opportunity to improve himself.
Equiano's master sent him to wait upon the Miss Guerins.
The Miss Guerins had treated Equiano with much kindness previously.
The Miss Guerins sent Equiano to school.
Servants of the Miss Guerins told Equiano he could not go to Heaven unless he was baptized.
This made Equiano very uneasy.
Equiano had some faint idea of a future state.
Equiano communicated his anxiety about baptism to the eldest Miss Guerin.
Equiano became a favourite of the eldest Miss Guerin.
Equiano pressed the eldest Miss Guerin to have him baptized.
The eldest Miss Guerin told Equiano he should be baptized.
The eldest Miss Guerin had previously asked Equiano's master to let him be baptized.
Equiano's master had refused baptism for Equiano initially.
The eldest Miss Guerin insisted on Equiano's baptism.
Equiano's master was under some obligation to Miss Guerin's brother.
Equiano's master complied with the request for Equiano's baptism.
Equiano was baptized in St. Margaret's church, Westminster, in February 1759.
Equiano was baptized under his present name.
The clergyman gave Equiano a book called a Guide to the Indians.
The book was written by the Bishop of Sodor and Man.
The eldest Miss Guerin stood as Equiano's godmother at his baptism.
The eldest Miss Guerin gave Equiano a treat after his baptism.
Equiano attended the Miss Guerins about the town.
Equiano was extremely happy in this service.
Equiano had many opportunities of seeing London, which he desired of all things.
Equiano was sometimes with his master at his rendezvous-house at the foot of Westminster-bridge.
Equiano enjoyed playing about the bridge stairs.
Equiano often played in the watermen's wherries with other boys.
While playing in a wherry, two stout boys came to Equiano and another boy in another wherry.
The two stout boys abused Equiano and the other boy for taking the boat.
The stout boys asked Equiano to get into their wherry-boat.
As Equiano was getting out of his wherry, the boys shoved it off.
Equiano fell into the Thames.
Equiano could not swim.
Equiano would have been drowned but for the assistance of watermen who came to his relief.
The Namur was got ready for sea again.
Equiano's master and his gang were ordered on board the Namur.
Equiano was obliged to leave his school-master, whom he liked very much.
Equiano left the Miss Guerins with uneasiness and regret.
The Miss Guerins taught Equiano to read.
The Miss Guerins took great pains to instruct Equiano in the principles of religion and knowledge of God.
Equiano parted from the Miss Guerins with reluctance.
The Miss Guerins gave Equiano friendly cautions about how to conduct himself.
The Miss Guerins gave Equiano valuable presents.
When Equiano came to Spithead, the fleet was destined for the Mediterranean.
A large fleet was ready to put to sea.
The admiral soon came on board.
About the beginning of spring 1759, the fleet weighed anchor and sailed for the Mediterranean.
In eleven days from the Land's End, the fleet reached Gibraltar.
While at Gibraltar, Equiano was often on shore.
Equiano got various fruits in great plenty and very cheap at Gibraltar.
Equiano had frequently told people about being kidnapped with his sister.
Equiano had expressed his anxiety for his sister's fate.
Equiano had expressed sorrow at never meeting his sister again.
One day on shore at Gibraltar, someone told Equiano he knew where Equiano's sister was.
Equiano believed this story immediately and agreed to go with the person.
Equiano's heart leaped for joy at the prospect of seeing his sister.
The person conducted Equiano to a black young woman who looked like his sister.
At first sight, Equiano thought the woman was his sister.
Upon talking to the woman, Equiano found her to be of another nation.
The Preston came in from the Levant while the fleet was at Gibraltar.
Equiano's master told him he would see his old companion Dick.
Dick had gone in the Preston when she sailed for Turkey.
Equiano was much rejoiced at the news of Dick's arrival.
The captain of the Preston came on board Equiano's ship immediately after arriving.
Equiano ran to inquire after Dick.
Equiano learned from the boat's crew that Dick was dead.
The boat's crew had brought Dick's chest and other things to Equiano's master.
Equiano's master gave Dick's things to Equiano.
Equiano regarded Dick's things as a memorial of his friend.
Equiano loved Dick and grieved for him as a brother.
While at Gibraltar, Equiano saw a soldier hanging by his heels at one of the moles.
Equiano thought this a strange sight compared to a man hanged by his neck in London.
Equiano saw the master of a frigate towed to shore on a grating by several men of war's boats.
The master of the frigate was discharged from the fleet, which Equiano understood was a mark of disgrace for cowardice.
On the same ship, Equiano saw a sailor hung up at the yard-arm.
After lying at Gibraltar for some time, the fleet sailed up the Mediterranean above the Gulf of Lyons.
One night the fleet was overtaken with a terrible gale of wind.
The gale was much greater than any Equiano had yet experienced.
The sea ran so high that the guns were in danger of getting loose despite being well housed.
If the guns had gotten loose, it would have proved the ship's destruction.
After cruising for a short time, the fleet came to Barcelona, a Spanish sea-port.
Barcelona was remarkable for its silk manufactures.
The ships were to be watered at Barcelona.
Equiano's master spoke different languages and often interpreted for the admiral.
Equiano's master superintended the watering of their ship.
Officers of other ships on the same service had tents pitched in the bay.
Spanish soldiers were stationed along the shore during the watering.
Equiano constantly attended his master.
Equiano was charmed with Barcelona.
While at Barcelona, it was like a fair with the natives bringing fruits of all kinds.
The natives sold fruits much cheaper than Equiano got them in England.
The natives brought wine down in hog and sheep skins, which diverted Equiano.
The Spanish officers treated the English officers with great politeness and attention.
Some Spanish officers came often to Equiano's master's tent to visit him.
The Spanish officers mounted Equiano on horses or mules so he could not fall.
The Spanish officers set the horses or mules off at full gallop with Equiano.
Equiano's imperfect skill in horsemanship afforded the Spanish officers entertainment.
After the ships were watered, the fleet returned to their old station of cruising off Toulon.
Equiano was charmed with the bay where tents were pitched.
Native people brought fruits and wine to sell to the British sailors.
Spanish officers treated British officers with great politeness and attention.
Spanish officers sometimes mounted Equiano on horses or mules for entertainment.
Equiano had imperfect skill in horsemanship.
The British fleet cruised off Toulon to intercept French men of war.
A battle occurred between British ships and two French frigates covered by batteries.
One French frigate sank during the engagement.
The British ships' topmasts were shot away in the battle.
Equiano later sailed with a man who fought in one of the French batteries.
The fleet arrived at Gibraltar around August 1759.
The French fleet passed through the straits while the British admiral was on shore.
Two captains left their ships in the confusion and came aboard Equiano's ship.
The British fleet chased the French fleet at night and into the next day.
Seven British ships engaged seven French ships in battle.
The British admiral fought the French with only his own division of seven ships.
The French commander was Monsieur La Clue in the ship Ocean.
The British admiral ordered his men to lie on their bellies until close to the Ocean.
The French line was broken and the British obtained victory.
The British captured three French prizes: La Modeste, Le Temeraire, and Centaur.
The Ocean and Redoubtable ran ashore at Cape Logas on the coast of Portugal.
The British set fire to both the Ocean and Redoubtable.
The Ocean exploded at midnight.
Equiano's station during the engagement was on the middle-deck.
Equiano and another boy brought powder to the aftermost gun.
Equiano witnessed many of his companions killed during the battle.
Equiano escaped the battle unhurt.
Equiano's master was wounded during the engagement.
Equiano dared not leave his post to assist his wounded master.
Equiano and his gun-mate ran great risk of blowing up the ship when powder spilled on deck.
Equiano initially thought it safest to fetch powder after the French fired their broadside.
Equiano cast off all fear of death and performed his duty with alacrity.
Equiano hoped to relate the battle to Miss Guerin and others in London.
Equiano's ship suffered greatly in the engagement with many killed and wounded.
The admiral appointed Equiano's master captain of the Ætna fire-ship.
Equiano left the Namur and went aboard the Ætna with his master.
Equiano liked the Ætna very much.
Equiano became the captain's steward on the Ætna.
Equiano was extremely well treated by all on board the Ætna.
Equiano had leisure to improve himself in reading and writing on the Ætna.
Equiano had learned reading and writing a little before leaving the Namur at a school on board.
The Ætna arrived at Spithead and went into Portsmouth harbour to refit.
The fleet was thought to be intended against Havannah.
The king died around the time of the planned expedition.
The ship was stationed at Cowes in the Isle of Wight until the beginning of 1761.
Equiano spent time pleasantly on shore in the Isle of Wight.
Equiano found the inhabitants of the Isle of Wight very civil.
Equiano met a black boy about his own size in a field on the Isle of Wight.
The black boy was transported at the sight of someone from his own country.
The black boy caught hold of Equiano as if he had been his brother.
Equiano was treated very kindly at the black boy's master's house.
Equiano and the black boy were very happy in frequently seeing each other.
The ship had orders to fit out again for another expedition around March 1761.
A large fleet at Spithead was commanded by Commodore Keppel.
The fleet was destined against Belle-Isle with transport ships carrying troops.
Equiano longed to engage in new adventures and see fresh wonders.
Equiano had a mind on which every uncommon thing made its full impression.
Equiano considered extraordinary escapes and signal deliverances to be effected by Providence.
A gunner named John Mondle was on board with very indifferent morals.
Mondle's cabin was between the decks, over where Equiano lay.
On the night of April 20th, Mondle awoke terrified from a dream.
Mondle dreamed he saw many awful things and was warned by St. Peter to repent.
Mondle said time was short in his dream.
Mondle made a vow never to drink strong liquors again.
Mondle gave away his sea-stores of liquor.
Mondle began to read the Scriptures seeking relief.
The ship was struck by the Lynne, a forty-gun ship captained by Captain Clark.
The Lynne struck Equiano's ship in the middle of Mondle's bed and cabin.
Mondle's cabin was destroyed and he nearly died from splinters.
Equiano regarded Mondle's preservation as an awful interposition of Providence.
The two ships swung alongside each other with grappling-irons caught.
Equiano's ship was in such shocking condition that everyone thought it would sink.
The lieutenant never quitted the ship during the crisis.
The captain encouraged the people to return and try to save the ship.
Ships from the fleet sent boats to assist in saving Equiano's ship.
It took the whole day to save the ship.
The ship was frapped together with many hawsers and tallow was used below water.
The ship arrived at Belle-Isle for repairs.
Mondle's escape had a great influence on his life and conduct afterwards.
Equiano belonged to the Jason, a fifty-gun ship, for a few days in 1758 at Plymouth.
A woman with a child at her breast fell from the upper-deck into the hold near the keel.
The woman and child were not hurt despite the fall.
Equiano fell headlong from the upper-deck of the Ætna down the after-hold.
Equiano received not the least injury from his fall.
A man fell from the mast-head on the deck of the Ætna without being hurt.
In 1758, Equiano belonged to the Jason, a ship of fifty-four guns, at Plymouth.
A woman with a child at her breast fell from the upper-deck of the Jason into the hold near the keel.
The woman and child who fell into the hold were not hurt.
Equiano fell headlong from the upper-deck of the Ætna down the after-hold when the ballast was out.
Equiano received no injury from his fall from the Ætna.
Equiano believed these miraculous escapes demonstrated God's intervention.
Equiano began to raise his fear from man to God alone.
Equiano called daily on God's holy name with fear and reverence.
Equiano's master was a junior captain who had a share in the command of a landing on April 8th.
The French were drawn up on the shore and opposed the landing of English men.
General Crawford was taken prisoner during the landing engagement.
A lieutenant was killed in the day's engagement.
On April 21st, English soldiers effected a safe landing and attacked the French.
Equiano's master was ordered on shore to superintend the landing of materials for a siege.
Equiano attended his master during the siege operations.
Equiano went to an English battery near the citadel walls to see how mortars were charged and shells were fired.
A large French shell burst within nine or ten yards of Equiano.
Equiano took shelter under a rock to avoid the fury of a shell burst.
Three shots were fired at Equiano and another boy.
An English sergeant reprimanded Equiano severely for passing the lines by stealth along the seashore.
The English sergeant took a sentinel into custody for negligence in allowing Equiano to pass.
Equiano mounted a French horse using a cord as a bridle.
A servant on an English horse helped Equiano by whipping his horse to make it go faster.
Equiano threw himself off the horse when it approached a craggy precipice and escaped unhurt.
Equiano determined not to be so foolhardy again after his escape.
The citadel was besieged until June, when it surrendered.
During the siege, Equiano counted above sixty shells and carcases in the air at once.
Equiano went through the citadel and the bomb-proofs cut in solid rock after it was taken.
After the taking of the island, English ships blocked up a French fleet at Basse-road.
English ships remained at Basse-road from June until February following.
Equiano saw many scenes of war and stratagems on both sides during the blockade.
The English attacked the French with ships of the line and boats, and frequently made prizes.
The French attacked English ships by throwing shells with bomb-vessels.
A French vessel broke from her springs behind the isle of I de Re and came within gun shot of the Nassau.
The Nassau could not bring a gun to bear upon the French vessel.
The English were twice attacked by French fire-floats that were chained together.
English boats with graplings towed the fire-floats safe out of the fleet.
Commodores Stanhope, Dennis, and Lord Howe commanded at Basse-road at different times.
Before the Spanish war began, Equiano's ship and the Wasp sloop were sent to St. Sebastian in Spain.
Commodore Dennis sent Equiano's ship as a cartel to Bayonne in France.
In February 1762, Equiano's ship went to Belle-Isle and stayed there until summer.
In September, Equiano's ship went to Guernsey.
Equiano saw his old hostess and her daughter in Guernsey.
Equiano spent time happily with his hostess and her daughter until October.
Equiano and his hostess parted with great affection.
Equiano promised to return soon to see them again.
Equiano's ship arrived at Portsmouth and remained there until late November.
In early December, Equiano's ship received orders to go to London to be paid off.
The crew received news of the ship going to London with loud huzzas and demonstrations of gladness.
Equiano thought of nothing but being freed and working for himself.
Equiano wanted to get money to enable him to get a good education.
Equiano always had a great desire to read and write.
While on shipboard, Equiano endeavoured to improve himself in reading and writing.
The captain's clerk of the Ætna taught Equiano to write.
The captain's clerk gave Equiano a smattering of arithmetic as far as the rule of three.
Daniel Queen, about forty years of age, was a well-educated man who messed with Equiano on the Ætna.
Daniel Queen dressed and attended the captain.
Daniel Queen became very much attached to Equiano.
Daniel Queen taught Equiano to shave and dress hair.
Daniel Queen taught Equiano to read in the Bible and explained passages to him.
Equiano was surprised to see the laws and rules of his country written in the Bible.
Equiano and Daniel Queen sat up whole nights together studying the Bible.
Daniel Queen was like a father to Equiano.
Some people called Equiano by Daniel Queen's name.
Some people styled Equiano 'the black Christian.'
Equiano almost loved Daniel Queen with the affection of a son.
Equiano denied himself things so that Daniel Queen might have them.
Equiano bought Daniel Queen sugar or tobacco with money he earned from shaving.
Daniel Queen said he and Equiano never should part.
Daniel Queen said he would instruct Equiano in his business when the ship was paid off.
Equiano's master had not promised him freedom.
Equiano had received assurances that his master had no right to detain him.
Equiano's master treated him with the greatest kindness.
Equiano's master reposed unbounded confidence in him.
Equiano's master paid attention to his morals.
Equiano's master would never suffer him to deceive him or tell lies.
Equiano's master told him that if he lied, God would not love him.
Equiano never supposed his master would detain him longer than he wished.
Equiano's ship sailed from Portsmouth for the Thames and arrived at Deptford on December 10th.
Equiano's master ordered the barge to be manned without warning.
Equiano's master forced him into the barge, saying he would take care he should not leave.
Equiano's master swore he would cut Equiano's throat if he moved out of his sight.
Equiano told his master he was free and could not be served so by law.
Equiano's master enraged at this declaration and sprang into the barge himself.
The tide had just turned downward when Equiano's master forced him into the barge.
Equiano's master resolved to put him on board the first vessel he could get to receive him.
The boat's crew pulled against their will and became quite faint.
The boat's crew told Equiano his master could not sell him and that they would stand by him.
Equiano's master asked several vessels to receive him, but they could not.
Below Gravesend, Equiano's master came alongside the Charming Sally, going to the West Indies.
The Charming Sally was commanded by Captain James Doran.
Equiano's master agreed with Captain Doran for Equiano.
Captain Doran asked Equiano if he knew him.
Captain Doran told Equiano he was now his slave.
Equiano told Captain Doran his master could not sell him to anyone.
Equiano had served his master many years.
Equiano's master had taken all his wages and prize-money.
Equiano received only one sixpence during the war.
Equiano had been baptized.
Equiano had heard a lawyer and others tell his master that no man has a right to sell him.
Captain Doran said Equiano talked too much English.
Captain Doran said he had a method on board to make Equiano behave well and be quiet.
Equiano's former sufferings in the slave-ship came to his mind.
Equiano told Captain Doran he hoped to get right in Heaven if not among men.
Equiano's master took away the only coat he had with him.
Equiano's master said if his prize-money had been 10,000 pounds, he had a right to it all.
Equiano had about nine guineas scraped together from trifling perquisites and little ventures.
Equiano hid his nine guineas lest his master should take them.
Equiano still hoped to make his escape to the shore.
Equiano's old shipmates told him not to despair and said they would get him back.
Equiano's shipmates said they would come to Portsmouth to him when they got their pay.
Equiano's hopes were baffled and his deliverance was far off.
Equiano's master concluded his bargain with Captain Doran and left in the boat.
Equiano followed his master with aching eyes as long as he could.
When his master was out of sight, Equiano threw himself on the deck with sorrow and anguish.
Equiano had scraped together about nine guineas from trifling perquisites and little ventures during his long sea-faring life.
Equiano hid his nine guineas to prevent his master from taking them.
Equiano hoped to make his escape to the shore.
Equiano's old shipmates told him not to despair and promised to get him back.
Equiano's shipmates promised to come to Portsmouth to retrieve him as soon as they could get their pay.
Equiano followed his master and the master's people with aching eyes as they departed in a boat.
When his master was out of sight, Equiano threw himself on the deck with his heart ready to burst with sorrow and anguish.
Equiano expected all his toils to end but was instead plunged into what he supposed was a new slavery.
Equiano wept very bitterly for some time after realizing his situation.
Equiano believed he must have done something to displease the Lord.
On the morning of arrival at Deptford, Equiano had rashly sworn that as soon as they reached London he would spend the day in rambling and sport.
Equiano's conscience smote him for his unguarded expression about spending a day in rambling and sport.
Equiano considered his present situation as a judgment of Heaven on account of his presumption in swearing.
Equiano acknowledged his transgression to God with contrition of heart.
Equiano poured out his soul before God with unfeigned repentance and earnest supplications.
Equiano believed God had hitherto shadowed him with the wings of his mercy.
Equiano believed God had brought him by his invisible but powerful hand the way he knew not.
Equiano's new master told him to behave himself well and do the business of the ship the same as any of the rest of the boys.
Equiano's new master told him he should fare the better for good behavior.
Equiano made his new master no answer when given these instructions.
When asked if he could swim, Equiano said no.
Equiano was made to go under the deck and was well watched.
Equiano tried every means he could devise amongst the people of the ship to get a boat from the shore.
A sailor on board took a guinea from Equiano on pretence of getting him a boat.
The sailor promised Equiano time after time that a boat was hourly to come off.
Equiano watched when the sailor had the watch upon deck, but never saw either the boat or his guinea again.
The sailor gave information to the mates of Equiano's intention to go off.
The sailor never told the mates that he had got a guinea from Equiano to procure his escape.
After the ship had sailed and the sailor's trick was made known to the crew, Equiano had satisfaction in seeing the sailor detested and despised by them all.
Equiano was still in hopes that his old shipmates would not forget their promise to come for him to Portsmouth.
Equiano's old shipmates came to Portsmouth the day before the ship sailed and sent him oranges and other tokens of their regard.
Equiano's old shipmates sent him word they would come off to him themselves the next day or the day after.
A lady who lived in Gosport wrote to Equiano that she would come and take him out of the ship.
The lady from Gosport had been once very intimate with Equiano's former master.
Equiano used to sell and take care of a great deal of property for the lady from Gosport in different ships.
The lady from Gosport always showed great friendship for Equiano and used to tell his master that she would take him away to live with her.
A disagreement took place between Equiano's former master and the lady from Gosport.
Another lady succeeded the lady from Gosport in Equiano's master's good graces.
The new lady appeared sole mistress of the Ætna and mostly lodged on board.
Equiano was not so great a favourite with the new lady as with the former lady.
The new lady had conceived a pique against Equiano on some occasion when she was on board.
The new lady did not fail to instigate Equiano's master to treat him in the manner he did.
On the morning of the 30th of December, the wind was brisk and easterly.
The Oeolus frigate made a signal for sailing to escort the convoy.
Before any of Equiano's friends had an opportunity to come off to his relief, the ship got under way.
Equiano was a prisoner on board the ship without hope.
Equiano kept his swimming eyes upon the land in a state of unutterable grief.
In one day's time Equiano lost sight of the wished-for land.
In the first expressions of his grief, Equiano reproached his fate and wished he had never been born.
Equiano was ready to curse the tide that bore them and the gale that wafted his prison.
Equiano called on death to relieve him from the horrors he felt and dreaded.
Equiano's turbulent emotions gave way to calmer thoughts.
Equiano perceived that what fate had decreed no mortal on earth could prevent.
The convoy sailed on without any accident for six weeks with a pleasant gale and smooth sea.
In February, the Oeolus ran down a brig, one of the convoy, and she instantly went down.
On the 13th of February 1763, from the mast-head, they descried Montserrat.
At the sight of Montserrat, a fresh horror ran through all Equiano's frame and chilled him to the heart.
Equiano's former slavery rose in dreadful review to his mind and displayed nothing but misery, stripes, and chains.
Equiano called upon God's thunder and his avenging power to direct the stroke of death to him rather than permit him to become a slave.
Equiano was made to help unload and load the ship.
Two of the sailors robbed Equiano of all his money and ran away from the ship.
Equiano had been so long used to a European climate that he felt the scorching West India sun very painful at first.
The dashing surf would toss the boat and the people in it frequently above high water mark.
Equiano's limbs were sometimes broken by the surf, or even attended with instant death.
Equiano was day by day mangled and torn.
About the middle of May, Captain Doran sent for Equiano ashore one morning.
Equiano was told by the messenger that his fate was then determined.
Equiano came to the captain with fluttering steps and trembling heart.
Equiano found Captain Doran with Mr. Robert King, a quaker and the first merchant in the place.
Captain Doran told Equiano that his former master had sent him there to be sold.
Equiano's former master had desired Captain Doran to get him the best master he could.
Captain Doran said Equiano was a very deserving boy.
Captain Doran said if he were to stay in the West Indies he would be glad to keep Equiano himself.
Captain Doran said he could not venture to take Equiano to London because he was very sure that when he came there he would leave him.
Equiano burst out crying and begged Captain Doran much to take him to England with him.
Captain Doran told Equiano he had got him the very best master in the whole island.
Captain Doran said he could sell Equiano to his own brother-in-law for a great deal more money than what he got from Mr. King.
Mr. King said the reason he had bought Equiano was on account of his good character.
Mr. King said he had not the least doubt of Equiano's good behaviour.
Mr. King said he did not live in the West Indies but at Philadelphia.
Mr. King said he was going to Philadelphia soon.
Equiano understood something of the rules of arithmetic.
Mr. King said when they got to Philadelphia he would put Equiano to school and fit him for a clerk.
Equiano left the gentlemen considerably more at ease in himself than when he came to them.
Equiano was very grateful to Captain Doran and even to his old master for the character they had given him.
Equiano found the character given to him of infinite service to him afterwards.
Equiano went on board again and took leave of all his shipmates.
The next day the ship sailed.
When the ship weighed anchor, Equiano went to the waterside and looked at her with a very wishful and aching heart.
Equiano followed the ship with his eyes and tears until she was totally out of sight.
Equiano was so bowed down with grief that he could not hold up his head for many months.
Equiano believed that if his new master had not been kind to him he would have died under his grief at last.
Equiano soon found that Mr. King fully deserved the good character which Captain Doran had given of him.
Mr. King possessed a most amiable disposition and temper.
Mr. King was very charitable and humane.
If any of Mr. King's slaves behaved amiss, he did not beat or use them ill but parted with them.
Mr. King's slaves were afraid of disobeying him.
Mr. King treated his slaves better than any other man on the island.
Mr. King was better and more faithfully served by his slaves in return.
By Mr. King's kind treatment, Equiano endeavoured to compose himself.
Equiano determined to face whatever fate had decreed for him with fortitude, though moneyless.
Mr. King soon asked Equiano what he could do.
Mr. King said he did not mean to treat Equiano as a common slave.
Equiano told Mr. King he knew something of seamanship.
Equiano could shave and dress hair pretty well.
Equiano could refine wines, which he had learned on shipboard.
Equiano could write.
Equiano understood arithmetic tolerably well as far as the Rule of Three.
Mr. King asked Equiano if he knew anything of gauging.
Equiano answered that he did not know anything of gauging.
Mr. King said one of his clerks should teach Equiano to gauge.
Mr. King dealt in all manner of merchandize.
Mr. King kept from one to six clerks.
Mr. King loaded many vessels in a year, particularly to Philadelphia.
Mr. King was born in Philadelphia.
Mr. King was connected with a great mercantile house in Philadelphia.
Mr. King had many vessels and droggers of different sizes which used to go about the island.
Mr. King had other vessels to collect rum, sugar, and other goods.
Equiano understood pulling and managing boats very well.
Equiano's first hard work set by Mr. King was pulling and managing boats.
In the sugar seasons, pulling and managing boats used to be Equiano's constant employment.
Equiano rowed the boat and slaved at the oars from one hour to sixteen in the twenty-four.
During his rowing work, Equiano had fifteen pence sterling per day to live on, though sometimes only ten pence.
Equiano's allowance was considerably more than was allowed to other slaves that used to work with him.
Other slaves that worked with Equiano belonged to other gentlemen on the island.
Those poor slaves had never more than nine pence per day and seldom more than six pence from their masters or owners.
Those poor slaves earned their masters three or four pisterines.
It is a common practice in the West Indies for men to purchase slaves though they have not plantations themselves.
Men in the West Indies let out their purchased slaves to planters and merchants at so much a piece by the day.
Men in the West Indies give what allowance they choose out of the produce of their slaves' daily work to their slaves for subsistence.
The allowance given to slaves for subsistence is often very scanty.
Equiano rowed boats and worked at the oars from one hour to sixteen hours in a twenty-four hour period.
Equiano received fifteen pence sterling per day to live on, sometimes only ten pence.
Equiano received considerably more pay than other slaves working on the island.
Equiano's master gave owners of hired slaves two and a half pisterines per day and provided food for them.
Equiano's master was considered a man of feeling by the slaves.
Equiano witnessed a countryman staked to the ground and nearly flogged one hundred times for a minor delay in payment.
Equiano knew a countryman who saved money through frugality and had a white man buy him a boat.
A governor seized the boat of Equiano's countryman without payment.
The governor later died in the King's Bench in England in great poverty.
Equiano's countryman escaped to England during the last war.
Equiano saw his countryman several times after he came to England.
Equiano was once let out to fit a vessel and received no food from either party.
Equiano told his master about the mistreatment and was removed from that work.
Equiano's master was obliged to send a white man with Equiano to estates that would not deliver goods to enslaved people.
Equiano's master paid white men six to ten pisterines a day to accompany Equiano.
